items.q: Which method of heat transfer cooks food in a microwave?
items.correct: Radiation — microwaves are electromagnetic radiation that makes polar water molecules vibrate, generating heat.
items.misconception: Microwaves cook food from the inside out.
items.why: Microwaves penetrate ~1–2 cm into food and excite water molecules — they cook outer regions first, conducting heat inward. The 'inside out' myth comes from feeling cool outer surfaces after microwaving (because steam carries heat out).

items.q: What is the difference between a food allergy and an intolerance?
items.correct: An allergy involves the immune system; intolerance does not.
items.misconception: They are the same with different severity.
items.random_wrong: Allergies are temporary, intolerances are permanent.
items.why: Students treat these as severity points on one spectrum. They have different mechanisms - allergies trigger immune (IgE) responses (potentially fatal anaphylaxis); intolerances are digestive (e.g., lactase deficiency).

items.q: What is the main function of dietary fibre?
items.correct: Aiding digestion and promoting bowel health.
items.why: Students think all carbohydrates give energy. Fibre is a carb humans can't digest, so it provides NO direct energy. It bulks stools, slows glucose absorption, and feeds gut bacteria.

items.q: Roughly how big is a veg portion (g)?
items.why: It's 80. “20” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
It's Palm size. “Fist size” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Roughly how many calories does an active KS3 student need a day?
items.why: Exact answer: 2200. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
